section.title: Jonathan's Victory
section.sectionRationale: Against Saul's paralysis, his son Jonathan acts in bold faith. With only his armor-bearer, he climbs to a Philistine outpost trusting that nothing can hinder the LORD from saving by many or by few, and God routs the enemy with a panic and an earthquake. But Saul nearly ruins the victory with a rash oath that curses anyone who eats, snaring Jonathan, who had tasted honey; only the people's protest saves him. It is one movement: the LORD saves through faith, while Saul's foolish leadership endangers the very deliverance God gives.
section.thesis: The living contrast between Jonathan's daring faith and Saul's fearful, rash leadership shows that salvation belongs to the LORD, who is not hindered by small numbers, while a self-centered king can imperil God's own people, so that the chapter both celebrates faith and deepens the case against Saul's kingship.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Jonathan's venture of faith, verses 1 to 15; the rout and the LORD's salvation, verses 16 to 23; Saul's rash oath and the honey, verses 24 to 35; the lot falls on Jonathan and the people rescue him, verses 36 to 46; and a summary of Saul's wars and house, verses 47 to 52.

units.label: Jonathan's venture of faith
units.note: nothing can hinder the LORD from saving, by many or by few

verses.text: One day Jonathan son of Saul said to the young man bearing his armor, “Come, let us cross over to the Philistine outpost on the other side.” But Jonathan did not tell his father.
Meanwhile, Saul was staying under the pomegranate tree in Migron on the outskirts of Gibeah. And the troops who were with him numbered about six hundred men,

including Ahijah, who was wearing an ephod. He was the son of Ichabod’s brother Ahitub son of Phinehas, the son of Eli the priest of the LORD in Shiloh. But the troops did not know that Jonathan had left.
Now there were cliffs on both sides of the pass that Jonathan intended to cross to reach the Philistine outpost. One was named Bozez and the other Seneh.
One cliff stood to the north toward Michmash, and the other to the south toward Geba.

Jonathan said to the young man bearing his armor, “Come, let us cross over to the outpost of these uncircumcised men. Perhaps the LORD will work on our behalf. Nothing can hinder the LORD from {{yasha|saving}}, whether by many or by few.”
His armor-bearer replied, “Do all that is in your heart. Go ahead; I am with you heart and soul.”
“Very well,” said Jonathan, “we will cross over toward these men and show ourselves to them.
If they say, ‘Wait until we come to you,’ then we will stay where we are and will not go up to them.
But if they say, ‘Come on up,’ then we will go up, because this will be our sign that the LORD has delivered them into our hands.”
So the two of them showed themselves to the outpost of the Philistines, who exclaimed, “Look, the Hebrews are coming out of the holes in which they were hiding!”

So the men of the outpost called out to Jonathan and his armor-bearer, “Come on up, and we will teach you a lesson!” “Follow me,” Jonathan told his armor-bearer, “for the LORD has delivered them into the hand of Israel.”
So Jonathan climbed up on his hands and feet, with his armor-bearer behind him. And the Philistines fell before Jonathan, and his armor-bearer followed and finished them off.
In that first assault, Jonathan and his armor-bearer struck down about twenty men in about half an acre of land.
Then panic struck the Philistines in the camp, in the field, and among all the people. Even those in the outposts and raiding parties trembled. Indeed, the earth quaked, and panic spread from God.

units.label: The rout and the LORD's salvation
units.note: so the LORD saved Israel that day

verses.text: Now when Saul’s watchmen at Gibeah in Benjamin looked and saw the troops melting away and scattering in every direction,
Saul said to the troops who were with him, “Call the roll and see who has left us.” And when they had called the roll, they saw that Jonathan and his armor-bearer were not there.
Then Saul said to Ahijah, “Bring the ark of God.” (For at that time it was with the Israelites.)
While Saul was talking to the priest, the commotion in the Philistine camp continued to increase. So Saul said to the priest, “Withdraw your hand.”
Then Saul and all his troops assembled and marched to the battle, and they found the Philistines in total confusion, with each man wielding the sword against his neighbor.
And the Hebrews who had previously gone up into the surrounding camps to join the Philistines now went over to the Israelites who were with Saul and Jonathan.
When all the Israelites who had been hiding in the hill country of Ephraim heard that the Philistines were fleeing, they also joined the battle in close pursuit.
So the LORD saved Israel that day, and the battle moved on beyond Beth-aven.

units.label: Saul's rash oath and the honey
units.note: cursed is the man who eats any food before evening

verses.text: Now the men of Israel were in distress that day, for Saul had placed the troops under an {{alah|oath}}, saying, “Cursed is the man who eats any food before evening, before I have taken vengeance on my enemies.” So none of the troops tasted any food.
Then all the troops entered the forest, and there was honey on the ground.
And when they entered the forest and saw the flowing honey, not one of them put his hand to his mouth, because they feared the oath.

Jonathan, however, had not heard that his father had bound the people with the oath. So he reached out the end of the staff in his hand, dipped it into the honeycomb, and put his hand to his mouth, and his eyes brightened.
Then one of the soldiers told him, “Your father bound the troops with a solemn oath, saying, ‘Cursed is the man who eats food today.’ That is why the people are faint.”
“My father has brought trouble to the land,” Jonathan replied. “Just look at how my eyes have brightened because I tasted a little of this honey.
How much better it would have been if the troops had eaten freely today from the plunder they took from their enemies! Would not the slaughter of the Philistines have been much greater?”
That day, after the Israelites had struck down the Philistines from Michmash to Aijalon, the people were very faint.
So they rushed greedily to the plunder, taking sheep, cattle, and calves. They slaughtered them on the ground and ate meat with the blood still in it.
Then someone reported to Saul: “Look, the troops are sinning against the LORD by eating meat with the blood still in it.” “You have broken faith,” said Saul. “Roll a large stone over here at once.”

Then he said, “Go among the troops and tell them, ‘Each man must bring me his ox or his sheep, slaughter them in this place, and then eat. Do not sin against the LORD by eating meat with the blood still in it.’” So that night everyone brought his ox and slaughtered it there.
Then Saul built an altar to the LORD; it was the first time he had built an altar to the LORD.

units.label: The lot falls on Jonathan and the people rescue him
units.note: the people rescued Jonathan, and he did not die

verses.text: And Saul said, “Let us go down after the Philistines by night and plunder them until dawn, leaving no man alive!” “Do what seems good to you,” the troops replied. But the priest said, “We must consult God here.”
So Saul inquired of God, “Shall I go down after the Philistines? Will You give them into the hand of Israel?” But God did not answer him that day.
Therefore Saul said, “Come here, all you leaders of the troops, and let us investigate how this sin has occurred today.
As surely as the LORD who saves Israel lives, even if it is my son Jonathan, he must die!” But not one of the troops said a word.
Then Saul said to all Israel, “You stand on one side, and I and my son Jonathan will stand on the other side.” “Do what seems good to you,” the troops replied.

So Saul said to the LORD, the God of Israel, “Why have You not answered Your servant this day? If the fault is with me or my son Jonathan, respond with {{urim|Urim}}, but if the fault is with the men of Israel, respond with Thummim.” And Jonathan and Saul were selected, but the people were cleared of the charge.
Then Saul said, “Cast the lot between me and my son Jonathan.” And Jonathan was selected.
“Tell me what you have done,” Saul commanded him. So Jonathan told him, “I only tasted a little honey with the end of the staff that was in my hand. And now I must die?”
And Saul declared, “May God punish me, and ever so severely, if you, Jonathan, do not surely die!”

But the people said to Saul, “Must Jonathan die, he who accomplished such a great deliverance for Israel? Never! As surely as the LORD lives, not a hair of his head will fall to the ground, for with God’s help he has accomplished this today.” So the people rescued Jonathan, and he did not die.
Then Saul gave up his pursuit of the Philistines, and the Philistines returned to their own land.

units.label: A summary of Saul's wars and house
units.note: wherever he turned, he routed them

verses.text: After Saul had assumed the kingship over Israel, he fought against all his enemies on every side, the Moabites, the Ammonites, the Edomites, the kings of Zobah, and the Philistines. Wherever he turned, he routed them.
He fought valiantly and defeated the Amalekites, delivering Israel from the hands of its plunderers.
Now the sons of Saul were Jonathan, Ishvi, and Malchishua. His two daughters were named Merab (his firstborn) and Michal (his younger daughter).
His wife’s name was Ahinoam daughter of Ahimaaz. The name of the commander of his army was Abner, the son of Saul’s uncle Ner.
Saul’s father Kish and Abner’s father Ner were sons of Abiel.
And the war with the Philistines was fierce for all the days of Saul. So whenever he noticed any strong or brave man, Saul would enlist him.

yasha.gloss: To save, deliver, rescue. Jonathan's creed, nothing can hinder the LORD from saving by many or by few, rests on this word, and the narrator confirms it: so the LORD saved Israel that day. Salvation is God's work, not a matter of numbers or strength; the same root stands behind the names Joshua and Jesus, the LORD who saves.

alah.gloss: An oath with a curse attached, a solemn adjuration. Saul binds the army with a rash alah, cursing anyone who eats, and it nearly costs his own son's life. The episode exposes self-centered, thoughtless leadership: an oath meant to serve Saul's vengeance instead weakens the army and endangers the innocent.

urim.gloss: Urim, with Thummim, sacred objects kept by the priest for seeking God's decision, often paired as a yes or no or a means of casting a holy lot. When God falls silent to Saul's inquiry, the Urim and Thummim single out Jonathan. God's guidance was available, but Saul's rash oath, not God's will, had created the crisis it exposed.

basic.context: While King Saul sits paralyzed under a tree with his small, frightened army, his son Jonathan quietly takes his armor-bearer and climbs up to a Philistine outpost, trusting that God can save by many or by few. God sends panic and an earthquake, the Philistines turn on each other, and Israel wins a great victory, so the LORD saved Israel that day. But Saul spoils the day with a foolish oath, cursing anyone who eats before evening. The exhausted troops go hungry, and Jonathan, who had not heard the oath, eats a little honey. When God goes silent, Saul hunts down the offender by lot, and it falls on Jonathan. Saul is ready to execute his own son for tasting honey, but the people intervene and rescue the hero. The question is: how does God save, and what kind of leader helps or hinders His work? God saves by faith, and Saul's self-centered folly nearly wrecks the rescue.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative built on contrast: Jonathan's bold, humble faith against Saul's fearful, rash, self-absorbed leadership. It is not simply commending reckless risk-taking; Jonathan acts on confidence in God, seeks a confirming sign, and gives God the glory. Saul's oath is a case study in leadership that serves the leader's pride rather than the people's good. The enduring truths are that salvation is God's and is not limited by numbers, and that leaders are accountable for how their words and vows affect others.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on the word yasha, to save: Jonathan trusts that nothing can hinder the LORD from saving by many or by few, and the narrator says the LORD saved Israel that day. Saul binds the army with a rash alah, an oath with a curse, that nearly kills his own son. And when God is silent, the urim and thummim expose the crisis Saul's own oath had made.
basic.typology: Jonathan, who wins a great deliverance and then stands condemned by his father's rash oath, only to be rescued by the people who declare he has worked salvation with God's help, faintly reflects gospel patterns: salvation accomplished, an innocent under an unjust sentence, and a deliverer vindicated. Above all, the creed that God saves by many or by few points to the God who saves not by human strength but by His own power in Christ.
basic.christ: Jonathan's confidence, nothing can hinder the LORD from saving, whether by many or by few, is the heartbeat of the gospel: God saves not by the strength of numbers or arms but by His own mighty hand, supremely at the cross, where salvation came through one man in apparent weakness. Jonathan, who accomplished a great deliverance yet was condemned by an oath he had not even heard, and then rescued by the people, offers a faint and imperfect echo of Christ, the true deliverer who was condemned though innocent, yet whose deliverance stands. And the name behind saved, yasha, is the very name Jesus, the LORD who saves.
basic.god: God is the Saviour who is not limited by numbers or weakness; He works through the faith of two men and sends panic and earthquake to rout an army. He is sovereign over the battle and the lot, guiding events to His end. He is holy, so that even a victorious army must not sin by eating blood. And He remains faithful to save His people even when their king's folly endangers them, showing that the deliverance is His and not the king's.
basic.crossReferences: Judges 7 shows God saving by Gideon's tiny band, by few not many. Zechariah 4 says not by might nor by power, but by My Spirit. 1 Corinthians 1 declares God saves through the weakness of the cross. And Matthew 1 gives the name Jesus, for He saves His people.
basic.oneStory: The LORD saves Israel through Jonathan's faith, proving that salvation depends on God, not numbers, while Saul's rash, self-serving leadership nearly destroys the very victory God gives. The contrast advances the book's case against Saul and lifts up the truth that God saves by faith and by His own power, a truth fulfilled in Christ, the LORD who saves His people.

context.literary: This chapter continues Saul's decline and sharpens it by contrast with Jonathan, whose faith recalls the judges and anticipates David. The rash oath and the near-execution of Jonathan expose Saul's flawed leadership. The closing summary of Saul's wars, verses 47 to 52, rounds off the account of his reign before the decisive rejection in chapter 15.
context.historical: The pass at Michmash with its steep cliffs, Bozez and Seneh, made Jonathan's climb daring and unexpected. Eating meat with the blood was forbidden by the law, Leviticus 17. The Urim and Thummim were priestly means of seeking God's decision. Battlefield oaths were known, but Saul's rash vow weakened his fighting force at the crucial moment, undermining the very victory it was meant to secure.
context.canonical: Jonathan's creed that God saves by many or by few joins Gideon's story and the wider biblical theme that deliverance is God's work, not human strength, reaching its climax in salvation through the weakness of the cross. Jonathan himself becomes David's devoted friend and covenant partner in the chapters that follow, a bond that runs through the rest of 1 Samuel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To celebrate faith that trusts God to save regardless of numbers, and to expose Saul's fearful, rash, self-centered leadership that endangers God's people, thereby advancing the book's case that the kingdom must pass to a better king.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Jonathan's venture and Saul's oath; it neither prescribes reckless raids nor commends rash vows. What endures is the truth that salvation is God's and is not bound by human strength, and the warning that leaders are responsible for the effect of their words and commitments on others.

debates.question: Was Jonathan's solo attack presumptuous or an act of true faith?

views.label: Genuine, God-centered faith
views.case: Jonathan grounds his venture in God's character, perhaps the LORD will work on our behalf, seeks a confirming sign before advancing, and gives God the glory for the victory; his boldness rests on confidence in God, not on self-reliance, so the narrative commends it.

views.label: Bold faith that still humbly submitted to God's will
views.case: The word perhaps and the requested sign show Jonathan did not presume upon God but left the outcome to Him; his was not a reckless dare but a faith willing to act while wholly dependent on the LORD's decision.

debates.bearing: Both readings present Jonathan as a model of faith that acts courageously while resting on God, in sharp contrast to Saul's fear and self-serving folly, and pointing to salvation that depends on God alone.

debates.question: What does Saul's rash oath reveal about his leadership?

views.label: Self-centered leadership that harms the people
views.case: Saul's oath is framed around his own vengeance, before I have taken vengeance on my enemies, and it starves and weakens his troops, reduces the victory, and nearly kills his own son; it exposes a king who serves himself rather than God's people.

views.label: Impulsive religiosity divorced from wisdom
views.case: Saul reaches for dramatic vows and inquiries but lacks the discernment to lead well; his religion is reactive and rash, so that even his zeal creates crises, and God's silence exposes that the problem lies with Saul, not the people.

debates.bearing: Either way the oath deepens the case against Saul's kingship, contrasting his folly with Jonathan's faith and preparing for the transfer of the kingdom to a king who serves God and His people.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is organized around three words. First and central is yasha, to save: Jonathan stakes everything on the creed that nothing can hinder the LORD from saving, whether by many or by few, and the narrator vindicates him, so the LORD saved Israel that day. Salvation is God's own act, never a function of numbers or weaponry, and this same root gives us the names Joshua and Jesus, the LORD who saves. Against this shining faith stands Saul's rash alah, an oath with a curse attached: cursed is the man who eats. Framed around Saul's own vengeance and made without wisdom, it starves the army, shrinks the victory, and nearly costs Jonathan his life. And when God falls silent to Saul's inquiry, the urim and thummim, the priestly means of seeking God's decision, single out Jonathan, exposing that the crisis was made not by God's will but by Saul's foolish oath. The vocabulary preaches the contrast: God saves by faith, while a self-serving king endangers the very people God delivers.

typology.type: Salvation by many or by few, not by human strength
typology.fulfillment: God saving His people through the apparent weakness of the cross, not by might, 1 Corinthians 1:25-31 and Zechariah 4:6.

typology.type: The deliverer condemned by an unjust sentence yet vindicated
typology.fulfillment: Christ, condemned though innocent, whose saving work nonetheless stands and is vindicated, Acts 3:14-15.

typology.type: The name behind saved, the LORD who saves
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, whose name means the LORD saves His people from their sins, Matthew 1:21.

study.christ: Jonathan's confidence is one of the great statements of faith in the Old Testament: nothing can hinder the LORD from saving, whether by many or by few. This is the very logic of the gospel, for God saves not by the strength of armies or numbers but by His own mighty hand, supremely at the cross, where the salvation of the world came through one man in apparent weakness and defeat, 1 Corinthians 1:25. Jonathan himself, who accomplished a great deliverance for Israel and yet was condemned to death by an oath he had never even heard, and then rescued by the people who testified that he had worked salvation with God's help, offers a faint and broken echo of Christ, the true Deliverer who was unjustly condemned though wholly innocent, yet whose saving work could not be undone. And the word at the chapter's heart, yasha, to save, is the very name Jesus, the LORD who saves His people. Where Saul's kingship endangers, Christ's kingship saves.
study.god: This chapter reveals God as the Saviour whose power is in no way limited by human weakness or small numbers. He works through the faith of two men against an outpost, then sends a panic and an earthquake to throw an entire army into self-destructive confusion, so that the victory is plainly His. He is sovereign over the battle and even over the sacred lot, guiding events unerringly to His purpose. He is holy, unwilling that even a victorious, famished army should sin by eating meat with the blood. And He remains faithful to save His people even when their own king's rash folly starves and endangers them, making unmistakable that the deliverance belongs to the LORD and not to Saul. This is the God who still saves by faith and by His own power, and who calls His people to trust Him rather than their numbers or their strength.

crossReferences.note: God saves through Gideon's tiny band so that Israel cannot boast, the same by few not many principle Jonathan trusts.
Not by might nor by power, but by My Spirit, says the LORD, the truth behind Jonathan's creed.
God saves through the weakness of the cross, so that no one may boast, the fullness of salvation by few.
The law forbidding the eating of blood, which the famished troops broke in their exhaustion after Saul's oath.

study.oneStory: The LORD saves Israel through Jonathan's daring faith, proving that salvation rests on God and not on numbers, while Saul's rash, self-serving oath nearly destroys the very victory God gives and almost kills the deliverer. The contrast sharpens the book's case against Saul and lifts up the enduring truth that God saves by faith and by His own power, a truth that comes to its fullness in Christ, the LORD who saves His people not by might but by the weakness of the cross.

quiz.q: What conviction drove Jonathan to attack the Philistine outpost with only his armor-bearer?

options.text: That nothing can hinder the LORD from saving, whether by many or by few.
options.note: Verse 6; Jonathan's faith rests on God's power, not on numbers.

options.text: That he and his armor-bearer were strong enough to win.
options.note: The opposite; Jonathan trusted God's saving power, not their own strength.

options.text: That Saul had ordered him to attack the outpost.
options.note: Jonathan acted on faith without telling his father, not on Saul's order.

options.text: That the Philistines were too weak to resist.
options.note: The Philistines were a vast force; Jonathan trusted God to save against the odds.

quiz.q: Who does the chapter credit for the victory over the Philistines?

options.text: The LORD, who saved Israel that day through Jonathan's faith and a God-sent panic.
options.note: Verses 15 and 23 attribute the deliverance to God, not human strength.

options.text: Jonathan and his armor-bearer, who struck the first blow.
options.note: They acted in faith, but the text credits the LORD, who sent panic and saved Israel.

options.text: Saul, whose leadership organized the rout.
options.note: Saul was passive and nearly hindered the victory; the LORD saved Israel.

options.text: The size and strength of the Israelite army.
options.note: Israel was few and frightened; the victory was God's, by many or by few.

quiz.q: How did Saul's rash oath affect the day?

options.text: It starved and weakened the troops, reduced the victory, and nearly caused his own son's death.
options.note: Verses 24 to 45; the self-centered oath harmed the people and endangered Jonathan.

options.text: It left the soldiers too faint to pursue the enemy fully.
options.note: True, but omits that it also nearly led to executing Jonathan, the deliverer.

options.text: It helped the army stay focused and win a greater victory.
options.note: The opposite; Jonathan said his father had brought trouble on the land.

options.text: It had no real effect on the battle.
options.note: The oath weakened the troops and endangered Jonathan, a significant effect.

quiz.q: How was Jonathan saved from his father's death sentence?

options.text: The people intervened, insisting he had worked a great deliverance with God's help, and rescued him.
options.note: Verse 45; the people vindicated the deliverer against Saul's rash vow.

options.text: Saul changed his mind and pardoned Jonathan himself.
options.note: Saul insisted Jonathan must die; it was the people who rescued him.

options.text: Jonathan escaped from the camp in secret.
options.note: He was openly rescued by the people, not by escaping.

options.text: God struck Saul so he could not carry out the sentence.
options.note: The people rescued Jonathan; there is no such striking of Saul here.

quiz.q: How does Jonathan's creed point forward to the gospel?

options.text: God saves by many or by few, and supremely through the apparent weakness of the cross, not by human strength.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 1; salvation is God's power, fulfilled in Christ, whose name means the LORD saves.

options.text: It shows that faith-filled people can accomplish great things.
options.note: True, but stops short of the gospel truth that God saves by His own power, not human strength.

options.text: It teaches that God only helps large, strong armies.
options.note: The opposite; God saves by few as readily as by many.

options.text: It has no connection to the gospel.
options.note: Salvation by God's power, not human strength, is the heart of the gospel.

questions: Jonathan acted because he believed nothing can hinder the LORD from saving by many or by few. Where is God calling you to step out in faith rather than being paralyzed by the odds?
Saul's self-centered oath harmed the very people he led. How do your words, commitments, and moods affect those around you, and are they serving others or yourself?
Jonathan sought a sign and gave God the glory, holding boldness and humility together. How can you be courageous in obedience while remaining wholly dependent on God?
The people recognized and defended the one who had worked deliverance. Who around you is quietly serving faithfully, and how can you honor rather than hinder them?
Salvation belongs to the LORD, accomplished at the cross in apparent weakness. How does trusting God's power rather than your own strength change the way you face your battles?

translationNotes.note: Panic spread from God renders a phrase the footnote also gives as a terrible panic; either way the earthquake and terror are attributed to God's action in the rout.
The fuller quotation with Urim and Thummim follows the Septuagint and Vulgate; the Masoretic Text has only a short phrase. The Urim and Thummim were priestly means of seeking God's decision.
The source has an em dash after Must Jonathan die; rendered here as a comma. The people's protest vindicates the deliverer against Saul's rash oath.
The source has an em dash before the Moabites; rendered here as a comma. The verse summarizes Saul's wars on every side before his final rejection in chapter 15.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 14:1-52. Prose narrative. Two em dashes, in verses 45 and 47, were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
